\title{Representation-Induced Spatial Structure in Culturally Clustered
Heritage Data:\\[4pt]
A Harmonic-Alignment Case Study with UNESCO World Heritage Sites}

\author{Seth Tenenbaum%
  \thanks{ORCID: \href{https://orcid.org/0009-0008-5797-2498}{0009-0008-5797-2498}.
  Correspondence: seth@fourthtemple.com. The author declares no competing interests.}\\[2pt]
  \small Independent Scholar, USA}

\date{}
\maketitle

% ==============================================================================
\begin{abstract}
% ==============================================================================

When coordinate-dependent scoring methods are applied to spatially clustered
cultural datasets, they systematically produce reproducible, phase-localized
alignment patterns --- even in the absence of any underlying invariant spatial
periodicity. The mechanism is general: geographic clustering causes corpus sites
to coincide with lattice nodes for favorable phase choices; optimization selects
that phase; and the scoring function rewards the resulting coincidence. The
interaction of these three ingredients --- clustering geometry, coordinate
framing, and scoring function --- constructs a stable alignment signal that is
empirically reproducible within the representation and absent outside it. This
paper demonstrates the mechanism using the UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World
Heritage corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$ sites). A harmonic-deviation scoring
framework selects a preferred frame near 3\textdegree{} spacing anchored to
the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor; three threshold-free diagnostics converge
on a stable representation-induced alignment signal
(Holm-adjusted $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestOne$, $\pHolmTestTwo$,
$\pHolmTestThree$), consistent across domed-monument subsets and a Wikidata
stupa corpus. The signal is entirely absent under invariant spectral methods.
The study offers a reproducible cautionary case for archaeological and
digital-humanities spatial analysis: stable alignment signals can reflect
the interaction of corpus clustering, coordinate framing, and scoring design
rather than intrinsic spatial periodicity.

\bigskip\noindent\textbf{Keywords:}
representation dependence, spatial statistics, harmonic alignment,
UNESCO World Heritage, coordinate-dependent scoring, cultural analytics,
methodological artefact, monument distribution

\end{abstract}

% ==============================================================================
\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:introduction}
% ==============================================================================

Spatial analysis in archaeology and digital humanities rests on a set of
analytical choices that are rarely made explicit: the coordinate system, the
reference frame, the scoring or distance function, and the scale at which
alignment is sought. Each of these choices shapes what patterns are visible in
the data. When a corpus is geographically clustered --- as most cultural
heritage datasets are --- the interaction between clustering geometry and
analytical framing can generate stable, statistically detectable alignment
signals that have no counterpart in the underlying spatial process. Recognizing
and documenting such representation-induced structures is as important as
discovering genuine spatial regularities.

This paper demonstrates the phenomenon using a case study. We apply a
harmonic-deviation scoring framework to the UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World
Heritage corpus and show that the framework produces a reproducible,
phase-localized alignment signal within the selected coordinate representation.
The signal is stable across sub-corpora and consistent across related datasets.
It does not appear under invariant spectral analysis. The mechanism is explicit:
the geographic clustering of Eurasian cultural heritage sites causes their
longitudes to coincide with lattice nodes under favorable coordinate frames;
the optimization over frame parameters selects the most favorable frame; and
the scoring function translates that coincidence into a measurable alignment
signal. The signal is therefore a property of the representation, not of the
data-generating process.

The methodological contribution is threefold. First, we provide a worked
example in which a coordinate-aligned statistic constructs a stable alignment
signal in a well-known public dataset, with full reproducibility. Second, we
characterize the alignment signal precisely: it is sensitive to phase and
spacing perturbations, absent under invariant methods, and stable within the
representation. Third, we draw out implications for spatial analysis practice
in cultural analytics, where clustered heritage corpora are common and
representation choices are consequential.

\paragraph{Scope and framing.}
We treat the identified alignment signal as the primary object of study, not
as a failed test of some substantive hypothesis. The signal is
\emph{empirically reproducible within the representation}: it appears
consistently across sub-corpora and is not a statistical artifact of a single
test. It is \emph{representation-induced} in the sense that it requires the
harmonic-deviation scoring framework to appear and is absent under methods
that do not impose a coordinate frame. Understanding why such signals arise
and how to detect them is a practical concern for any researcher using
coordinate-aligned statistics on spatially structured datasets.

\paragraph{Paper structure.}
Section~\ref{sec:background} briefly reviews prior work on spatial periodicity
in monument distributions and introduces the harmonic scoring class.
Section~\ref{sec:methods} describes the data and methods.
Section~\ref{sec:results} presents the results in four subsections: emergence
of the alignment signal, cross-corpus stability, representation dependence,
and robustness. Section~\ref{sec:interpretation} interprets why the signal
arises and what it reveals about the framework. Section~\ref{sec:implications}
draws implications for cultural analytics practice.
Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Background}
\label{sec:background}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Spatial Periodicity in Monument Distributions}

The statistical evaluation of spatial periodicity in monument distributions has
a long and contested history \citep{thom1967,kendall1974,freeman1976}.
Probability-based frameworks for archaeoastronomical alignments were developed
by Magli \citep{magli2013,magli2016} and by Ruggles and Silva
\citep{ruggles1999astronomy,ruggles2015handbook,silva2020statistical};
the ICOMOS-IAU thematic study \citep{ruggles2010icomos} provides institutional
context for World Heritage analysis. A recurring methodological challenge in
this literature is distinguishing intrinsic spatial periodicities from
alignment signals induced by the scoring method, the corpus structure, or the
choice of reference frame. The present study addresses this challenge directly
by taking representation dependence itself as the object of study.

\subsection{Harmonic-Deviation Scoring}

A \emph{harmonic-deviation score} measures alignment by computing, for each
site, the angular distance to the nearest node of a candidate lattice. Given
a spacing $\omega$ and a reference longitude $\phi$, the harmonic nodes fall
at $\phi + k\omega$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$; the deviation for a site at
longitude $\lambda$ is $\delta = |\lambda - \phi| \bmod \omega$, folded
to $[0, \omega/2]$. Sites near nodes receive small $\delta$; sites near
anti-nodes receive large $\delta$. A scoring function then aggregates
individual deviations into a corpus-level alignment index.

This class of statistics is \emph{coordinate-dependent} by construction: the
score depends on the imposed lattice, not on any intrinsic property of the
point distribution. A coordinate-dependent statistic produces high alignment
scores in clustered datasets even when the clustering is unrelated to the
lattice period, because the lattice nodes fall near at least some of the
clusters by chance, and the optimization over $(\omega, \phi)$ selects the
frame that maximizes this coincidence.

\subsection{Selected Reference Phase: the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E Corridor}

The global anchor sweep identifies the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor as the
highest-scoring phase for the UNESCO corpus. Both Gerizim
(\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E) and Jerusalem (\JerusalemLon\textdegree{}E) ---
landmarks \GerizimJerusalemKm{} km apart --- independently place this corridor
in the global top \anchorSweepTopPct\% of \anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors;
the corridor's rank is a property of the corpus geometry, not of any specific
landmark within it. We label the reference phase by Mount Gerizim because it
is not itself inscribed in the UNESCO list (tentative ref.~5706) and therefore
does not self-exclude from the corpus. It is used throughout as a geographic
label for the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E phase band, not as a causal or historical
explanation of the observed alignment signal. Results are anchor-invariant
within the \GerizimJerusalemKm{} km corridor window; the enrichment belongs to
the corridor, not to any individual landmark.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Data and Methods}
\label{sec:methods}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Corpus}

The UNESCO World Heritage XML dataset \citep{unescowhc2024xml} contains
$\NwhcTotal$ inscribed nominations. We retain Cultural and Mixed sites
(excluding $\NexcNatural$ Natural-only), drop $\NexcNoCoord$ entries lacking
geocoordinates (both linear route networks), yielding $N = \NclusterTotal$
sites. The corpus over-represents Europe and the Mediterranean-to-South-Asian
corridor (Europe and North America: \CorpusPctEurope\%; Asia-Pacific:
\CorpusPctAsiaPac\%; Latin America and Caribbean: \CorpusPctLatAm\%;
Arab States: \CorpusPctArabStates\%; Africa: \CorpusPctAfrica\%). This
geographic concentration is itself a structural feature that interacts with
the harmonic scoring, as we demonstrate in Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence}.

\subsection{Harmonic-Deviation Scoring and Frame Selection}

For anchor longitude $\phi =$ \GerizimLon\textdegree{}E and spacing
$\omega = 3$\textdegree{}, the deviation of site $i$ from the nearest
harmonic node is:
\[
  \delta_i = \bigl|(\lambda_i - \phi)\bmod \omega\bigr|,
  \quad \text{folded to } [0,\, \omega/2].
\]
The frame $(\omega, \phi) = (3\degr{}, \text{Gerizim})$ was selected by a
global parameter sweep: a scan over \anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors
(0\textdegree{} to 360\textdegree{}E, 0.01\textdegree{} resolution) and a
unit sweep over candidate spacings (0.5\textdegree{} to 15\textdegree{})
both maximize the harmonic-deviation enrichment score at 3\textdegree{}
spacing and the 35\textdegree{}E corridor. Permutation calibration of the
anchor sweep yields $p = \anchorMaxPermP$ (\anchorMaxPermPSig{}) after full
search correction. All subsequent analyses are conditional on this selected
frame (post-selection).

\subsection{Diagnostic Tests}
\label{sec:tests}

Three threshold-free diagnostics characterize the alignment signal at the
selected frame. They constitute the primary evidential family (Holm step-down
adjustment, $k = \BonfK$).

\textbf{Test~1 (dome von~Mises mixture).} A two-component von~Mises/uniform
mixture is fit to the harmonic phase deviations of the domed-monument
sub-corpus ($N = \NcircTotal$; see Section~\ref{sec:dome_subset}), with the
mean fixed at the Gerizim phase. The mixture weight $\hat{w}$ quantifies the
fraction of dome sites concentrated near harmonic nodes within the selected
representation; significance is assessed by parametric bootstrap.

\textbf{Test~2 (Spearman cluster asymmetry).} Spearman rank correlation
between harmonic node mean deviation and site count, full corpus
($N = \NclusterTotal$), permutation null. Tests whether nodes drawing
more sites show smaller mean deviations --- a signature of the cluster-node
interaction under the harmonic-deviation scoring framework.

\textbf{Test~3 (phase-rotation null).} The reference grid is shifted by a
uniform random offset in $[0\degr{}, 3\degr{})$; the test asks whether
the Gerizim phase minimizes the full-corpus mean harmonic deviation
relative to random grid placements within the selected representation.

Supporting enrichment tests (Tests~4 and~5) use tier-threshold windows and
are not in the primary diagnostic family; they provide descriptive context.

\subsection{Tier Classification}
\label{sec:tiers}

Sites are assigned to descriptive tiers by their deviation $\delta$:
Tier-A++ ($\delta \leq \AppThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\AppThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateApp$),
Tier-A+ ($\delta \leq \ApThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\ApThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateAp$),
Tier-A ($\delta \leq \ATierThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\ATierThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateA$).
Tiers are descriptive vocabulary within the harmonic-deviation representation;
no primary diagnostic depends on a tier cutoff.

\subsection{Domed-Monument Sub-corpus}
\label{sec:dome_subset}

A keyword filter (\textit{stupa/stupas}, \textit{tholos},
\textit{dome/domed/domes}, \textit{spherical}) applied to site names and
UNESCO description texts yields $N = \NcircTotal$ sites. The keyword set was
refined iteratively during analysis; results from this sub-corpus are
hypothesis-generating, not confirmatory. A context-validated subset
($N = \NcircValidated$, \NcircContextRejected{} ambiguous sites rejected)
is used for sensitivity checks.

\subsection{External Corpus: Wikidata Q180987 Stupas}

A separately assembled Wikidata corpus of $N = \wikiStupaTotal$ stupa sites
(class Q180987) \citep{tenenbaum2026audit} is analyzed under the identical
anchor, spacing, and null model. This corpus overlaps geographically and
culturally with UNESCO; it serves as a shared-representation consistency
check, not independent replication.

\subsection{Simulation Nulls}

Three simulation approaches (\simNperms{} trials each, seed~$= \simSeed{}$):
(1) longitude permutation within the corpus,
(2) bootstrap from the full corpus,
(3) KDE-smoothed null modeling the observed longitude density.
A restricted geographic-concentration null (Null~C) additionally draws from
all full-corpus sites within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome site, providing a
footprint-matched reference distribution for the dome alignment signal.

\subsection{Reproducibility}

All statistics are generated by an automated pipeline; macros are regenerated
without manual editing. Scripts, data, and reproduction instructions are
deposited at Zenodo (DOI: \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19938283},
v1.6.0) and catalogued in Supplementary~S1.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Emergence of the Alignment Signal under the Scoring Framework}
\label{sec:results_emergence}

Under the selected frame, the full UNESCO corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$)
shows a Tier-A+ rate of \clusterApRate\%
($\NclusterAp/\NclusterTotal$; Clopper-Pearson 95\% CI
[\clusterApCIlo\%, \clusterApCIhi\%]), against the \NullRateAp{} geometric
null (Figure~\ref{fig:devhist}). The enrichment is
$\clusterEnrichAp\times$ the null rate, measured within the selected
harmonic-deviation representation.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{../figures/fig_devhist}
\caption{Harmonic deviation distribution for the full UNESCO corpus
  ($N = \NclusterTotal$) under the coordinate-dependent scoring framework.
  The shaded region marks the Tier-A+ window
  ($\delta \leq \ApThreshDeg\degr{}$); the dashed line marks the
  \NullRateAp{} geometric null. The excess of sites near harmonic nodes
  is the alignment signal characterized throughout this paper. It emerges
  from the interaction of geographic clustering with the imposed harmonic
  frame and is absent under invariant spectral methods
  (Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence}).}
\label{fig:devhist}
\end{figure*}

The 3\textdegree{} spacing is the highest-scoring parameter from the unit
sweep. A phase-envelope scan (\phaseNphases{} phases at
\phaseStep\textdegree{} resolution) demonstrates strong phase localization
within the representation: full-corpus A+ counts span \phaseFullMin{} to
\phaseFullMax{} (median \phaseFullMedian{}), and the Gerizim phase sits at
the \phaseFullGerPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile (\phaseFullGerCount{}
A+). Rayleigh permutation on the 3\textdegree{}-circle:
$p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \rayleighPermP$ (\rayleighPermPSig{}).

The three primary diagnostics all detect a consistent alignment signal within
the selected harmonic representation (Table~\ref{tab:primary_tests}).
\textbf{Test~1} (dome von~Mises mixture):
$\hat{w} = \targetedWdome$ ($\approx\targetedWdomePct\%$ of dome sites
concentrated at the Gerizim phase under the harmonic-deviation framework;
$\hat{\kappa} = \targetedKdome$, angular spread
$\approx\targetedSdDomeDeg\degr{}$),
$p_{\mathrm{boot}} = \targetedPdome$ (\targetedPdomeSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestOne$, \pHolmTestOneSig{}).
\textbf{Test~2} (Spearman cluster asymmetry):
$\rho = \spearmanDevClusterRho$, permutation
$p = \spearmanDevClusterP$ (\spearmanDevClusterPSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestTwo$, \pHolmTestTwoSig{}).
Nodes drawing more sites show systematically smaller mean deviations,
the expected signature of cluster-node coincidence under the scoring
framework.
\textbf{Test~3} (phase-rotation null): the Gerizim phase minimizes the
full-corpus mean harmonic deviation relative to random grid placements
($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullMeanDevPermP$, \fullMeanDevPermPSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestThree$, \pHolmTestThreeSig{}).

\begin{table}[!t]
\centering
\caption{Primary diagnostic family ($k = \BonfK$, Holm step-down).
  All three tests operate within the selected harmonic representation;
  adjusted $p$-values quantify the strength of the alignment signal
  within the selected frame.}
\label{tab:primary_tests}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lcrll}
\toprule
Test & $N$ & Raw $p$ & Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}}$ & Direction \\
\midrule
Test~1 (dome VM mix.)
  & \NcircTotal & \targetedPdome\textsuperscript{b}
  ($w\!=\!\targetedWdome$, $\kappa\!=\!\targetedKdome$)
  & \pHolmTestOne~\pHolmTestOneSig{} & Positive \\
Test~2 (Spearman $\rho$)
  & \NclusterTotal & \spearmanDevClusterP\textsuperscript{b}
  ($\rho\!=\!\spearmanDevClusterRho$)
  & \pHolmTestTwo~\pHolmTestTwoSig{} & Positive \\
Test~3 (mean dev.)
  & \NclusterTotal & \fullMeanDevPermP\textsuperscript{b}
  & \pHolmTestThree~\pHolmTestThreeSig{} & Positive \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{5}{p{0.9\textwidth}}{\footnotesize
  \textsuperscript{b}permutation or bootstrap $p$.
  Holm (1979) step-down \citep{holm1979simple}.}
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The domed-monument sub-corpus shows a stronger alignment signal than the full
corpus under the same framework: \NcircTierAp/\NcircTotal{} sites at Tier-A+
(\NcircApRate\%, $\circEnrichAp\times$; Fisher $p = \pCircApFisher$
\pCircApFisherSig{}).
Tier-A++ concentration is more pronounced: \NcircTierApp/\NcircTotal{}
($\NcircAppRate\%$, $\circEnrichApp\times$ the \NullRateApp{} null;
permutation $p = \circAppPermP$ \pCircAppPermSig{};
bootstrap $p = \circAppBootP$ \pCircAppBootSig{}).

\paragraph{Sites with high harmonic proximity.}
Tier-A+ domed monuments under the selected frame include Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
(0.2~km), Boyana Church (0.3~km), Lumbini (0.8~km), Humayun's Tomb (2.0~km),
Pyu Ancient Cities (2.3~km), Silk Roads: Chang'an--Tianshan (2.5~km),
Trulli of Alberobello (3.6~km), Old City of Jerusalem (4.1~km),
Borobudur (4.3~km), Kizhi Pogost (5.0~km), and Echmiatsin/Zvartnots (6.2~km).
These distances are harmonic deviations within the selected representation;
they span the Eurasian corridor from the Levant to Java, sharing geographic
and cultural structure rather than constituting independent evidence of
spatial periodicity.

\subsection{Stability across Subsets and Corpora}
\label{sec:results_stability}

The alignment signal is reproducible across multiple sub-populations and an
external corpus, within the selected harmonic-deviation representation.

\paragraph{Leave-$n$-out stability.}
No single site drives the dome alignment signal: removing any A+ site yields
$N = \LOOdomeN$, A+$= \LOOdomeAp$, $p = \LOOdomeP$.
Leave-3-out worst case across all $\binom{\LKOnAp}{3}$ triples:
$p = \LKOworstThreeP$. The A++ sub-population is more robust still:
leave-3-out worst case $p = \LKOAppworstThreeP$ (leave-2-out: $p = \LKOAppworstTwoP$).

\paragraph{Multi-scale enrichment.}
A Fisher combination across \multiscaleK{} log-spaced proximity windows
(null rates 0.33\% to 40\%) finds all windows enriched above their null
within the representation (OR $1.2\times$ to $6.7\times$; permutation-calibrated
$p = \multiscaleDomeCombinedP$ \multiscaleDomeCombinedPSig{}).
Applied jointly across the UNESCO full corpus, UNESCO domes, Wikidata
stupas, and OWTRAD route nodes under the same harmonic frame, the maximum
joint enrichment peaks near \multiscaleJointMaxKm{}~km from a harmonic node
($p = \multiscaleJointMaxP$ \multiscaleJointMaxPSig{}), a scale that
emerges from the data rather than any pre-specified threshold.

\paragraph{Wikidata Q180987 stupa corpus.}
Under the identical anchor, spacing, and null, $N = \wikiStupaTotal$
Wikidata-attested stupas yield \wikiStupaATierCount/\wikiStupaTotal{} Tier-A
sites (\wikiStupaATierRate\%, $\wikiStupaATierEnrich\times$ null;
binomial $p\ \wikiStupaATierBinomP$ \wikiStupaATierBinomStar;
permutation $p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \wikiStupaPermP$ \wikiStupaPermStar).
Harmonic nodes with at least one Tier-A stupa carry $\wikiStupaClusterRatio\times$
more stupa sites on average than empty nodes, mirroring the cluster-asymmetry
alignment signal of Test~2. The Java/Sumatra node (105\textdegree{} to
115\textdegree{}E) concentrates \wikiJavaTightATierN/\wikiJavaTightN{} sites
at Tier-A (\wikiJavaTightATierRate\%; OR $= \wikiJavaTightATierOR\times$,
$p\ \wikiJavaTightATierP$ \wikiJavaTightATierPSig{}).

This cross-corpus consistency confirms that the alignment signal is a stable
property of the harmonic scoring framework applied to geographically related
Eurasian corpora. It does not constitute independent replication, because
the corpora share geographic and cultural structure; it demonstrates that
the representation-induced signal is robust across related datasets under a
common scoring framework.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/fig_geo_trail}
\caption{Tier distribution of UNESCO domed/stupa and Wikidata Q180987 stupa
  sites along the Eurasian corridor under the harmonic-deviation scoring
  framework, overlaid on OWTRAD attested trade routes
  \citep{ciolek2004owtrad}. The alignment signal localizes to the Eurasian
  high-density corridor, consistent with geographic clustering producing
  an elevated score under the harmonic frame.}
\label{fig:geo_trail}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Representation Dependence}
\label{sec:repn_dependence}

The alignment signal requires the harmonic-deviation representation to appear.
Three observations establish representation dependence.

\paragraph{Absence under invariant methods.}
The full-corpus Rayleigh test for 3\textdegree{} periodicity returns
$R = \fullRayleighR$ ($Z = \fullRayleighZ$,
$p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$ \fullRayleighPermPSig{}): no
spectral periodicity at 3\textdegree{} is detectable in the longitude
distribution. The alignment signal does not appear as an intrinsic
periodicity; it appears only when the harmonic-deviation scoring function
is applied with a specific phase, confirming that it is a property of the
representation rather than the data-generating process.

\paragraph{Phase sensitivity.}
A $\pm 0.3\%$ shift from exactly 3\textdegree{} spacing collapses joint
significance across the dome sub-population, showing that the alignment
signal is sharply phase-dependent within the coordinate-dependent
representation. The phase-envelope scan demonstrates that most grid
placements do not produce the observed signal: the Gerizim phase sits at
the \phaseFullGerPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile of A+ counts across
all tested phases, well above the median (\phaseFullMedian{} A+).

\paragraph{Clustered-control simulation.}
A clustered-control experiment assesses whether the unconstrained global
maximum over all $(\omega, \phi)$ is typical for corpora with realistic
dome-site clustering. The result is affirmative ($p = \clusterCtrlPermP$,
$N_{\mathrm{sim}} = \clusterCtrlNsim$): the free-sweep maximizer returns
$\omega = \clusterCtrlObsOmega\degr{}$, a spacing with no metrological or
waypoint interpretation. The unconstrained maximum is uninformative because
the enrichment statistic uses a fixed absolute threshold and therefore
inflates at large spacings regardless of any real structure. The metrologically
motivated prior $\omega = 3\degr{}$ is tested conditionally, not recovered by
unconstrained search.

Together, these three findings establish that the alignment signal is a
property of the representation. It arises from the interaction of the scoring
function with the geographic clustering of Eurasian cultural heritage sites
under a specific coordinate frame, and does not reflect the underlying spatial
generative process.

\subsection{Robustness Checks}
\label{sec:results_robustness}

\paragraph{Geographic-concentration null.}
Domed monuments are concentrated in the Eurasian corridor
(\domeEurasianFraction\% in 20\textdegree{} to 120\textdegree{}E versus
\fullCorpusEurasianFraction\% of the full corpus). To assess whether this
footprint explains the alignment signal, we draw from all full-corpus sites
within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome longitude (Null~C) and compare dome A+
counts under the harmonic frame to this footprint-matched distribution.
Dome sites exceed the broader footprint at all tested windows:
$\pm 2\degr{}$ ($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwo$,
\geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwoSig{}); $\pm 5\degr{}$
($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$, \geoNullDomeRestrictedPSig{}); $\pm 10\degr{}$
($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPten$, \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtenSig{}).
Architectural type contributes to the alignment signal beyond longitude
footprint alone, even within the harmonic representation. This does not
change the representation-dependence interpretation: the enrichment relative
to the footprint still requires the harmonic frame to be quantified.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{../figures/fig_null_c}
\caption{Null~C bootstrap distributions for the context-validated dome
  sub-population ($N = \NcircValidated$, observed A+ $= \NcircValidatedAp$)
  at three window widths. For each window $\pm W\degr{}$, all full-corpus
  sites within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome longitude form the restricted
  pool; $10^5$ draws of size $N = \NcircValidated$ from that pool define the
  expected A+ distribution (blue bars). The observed count (red line) exceeds
  the pool mean at all three windows ($\pm 2\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwo$; $\pm 5\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$; $\pm 10\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPten$).}
\label{fig:null_c}
\end{figure*}

\paragraph{Anchor invariance within the corridor.}
Both corridor landmarks produce the same alignment signal: Gerizim
(\GerizimSweepAp{} A+, \anchorSweepPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile)
and Jerusalem (\JerusalemSweepAp{} A+,
\JerusalemSweepPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile) each place the
$\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor in the global top \anchorSweepTopPct\% of
\anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors. Candidate anchors outside the corridor
(Mecca, Mt.~Meru, Mt.~Kailash) do not show comparable enrichment, confirming
that the signal is a property of the corridor phase within the selected
representation, not of the specific reference label chosen.

\paragraph{Americas negative control.}
The UNESCO Americas sub-corpus ($N = \AmericasN$) shows an A+ rate of
\AmericasApRate\%, \emph{below} the \NullRateAp{} null
($p = \AmericasOneSidedP$ \AmericasOneSidedStar{}, one-sided depletion).
The alignment signal is absent under the harmonic-deviation framework in a
corpus without Eurasian cultural clustering, consistent with the
representation-dependence account.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Interpretation}
\label{sec:interpretation}
% ==============================================================================

The results present a clear empirical picture: a coordinate-dependent scoring
framework produces a reproducible, phase-localized alignment signal in the
UNESCO corpus and in related Eurasian datasets under the same representation;
the same signal is absent under invariant spectral methods. This section
explains the mechanism and draws out what the signal reveals.

\subsection{Why the Signal Appears: Cluster-Frame Interaction}

The harmonic-deviation framework creates a mapping from each site's longitude
to a deviation score. When the corpus is geographically clustered --- as the
UNESCO corpus is, with the Eurasian corridor hosting the great majority of
Cultural sites --- the imposed lattice will, for some phase choices, align
its nodes with the cluster centroids. When that alignment occurs, the
population of sites near cluster centroids receives small deviation scores,
producing the observed enrichment within the representation.

This mechanism has three ingredients:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Geographic clustering.} The UNESCO corpus clusters in the Eurasian
corridor because World Heritage inscription reflects historical and institutional
patterns in cultural heritage designation, not a uniform spatial process.
Domed monuments cluster more strongly than the full corpus (\domeEurasianFraction\%
vs. \fullCorpusEurasianFraction\% in the Eurasian core), amplifying the effect.

\item \textbf{Coordinate framing.} The harmonic lattice is defined in geographic
longitude, an arbitrary coordinate system from the perspective of cultural
processes. Different coordinate systems (e.g., distance from a different
reference point, projected coordinates, spherical harmonic decomposition)
would produce different alignment pictures. The alignment signal is specific
to the longitude frame.

\item \textbf{Scoring function.} The nearest-harmonic-deviation score treats
all sites symmetrically and rewards proximity to any lattice node. It is
sensitive to the imposed spacing and phase but insensitive to the global
periodicity of the point process. The full-corpus Rayleigh test, which
measures intrinsic periodicity rather than lattice proximity, detects no
3\textdegree{} signal ($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$,
\fullRayleighPermPSig{}).
\end{enumerate}

The joint action of these three ingredients produces the observed pattern: a
stable, phase-specific alignment signal that is empirically reproducible within
the representation and absent outside it. Critically, the signal arises from
the representational properties of the scoring framework --- the interaction
of corpus clustering, coordinate framing, and scoring function --- and does
not reflect any intrinsic periodicity in the underlying spatial process
generating heritage site locations.

\subsection{Reproducibility Does Not Imply Intrinsic Structure}

The alignment signal's stability across subsets and corpora can be mistaken
for evidence of an underlying spatial regularity. The stability is genuine:
leave-$n$-out checks, multi-scale enrichment, and cross-corpus consistency
all confirm it. But stability under resampling tests the robustness of the
\emph{framework-induced} alignment signal, not the existence of a
representation-independent periodicity. An alignment signal can be highly
reproducible within a representation and entirely coordinate-specific. The
present results are an example.

The distinction matters because resampling is the standard tool for assessing
robustness in spatial archaeology. Permutation tests and bootstrap procedures
establish that the enrichment exceeds what would be expected under longitude
randomization within the corpus --- but they assume the corpus structure
(geographic clustering, cultural affiliations, corpus composition) is fixed.
They do not test whether the signal would appear in a corpus without
Eurasian clustering, and the Americas negative control shows it does not.

\subsection{What the Signal Reveals}

This coordinate-dependent alignment signal is informative in two ways. First,
it reveals properties of the corpus: the Eurasian cultural heritage record is
strongly clustered, domed monuments cluster more sharply than the full corpus,
and this clustering interacts predictably with coordinate-aligned scoring.
Second, it provides a worked example of how a common class of spatial
statistics --- nearest-landmark or nearest-node deviation scores --- produces
stable, statistically detectable alignment signals in clustered datasets
without any underlying spatial periodicity. Recognizing this behavior is a
practical requirement for applied spatial analysis in cultural heritage
research.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Implications for Cultural Analytics}
\label{sec:implications}
% ==============================================================================

The UNESCO case study carries broader methodological implications for spatial
analysis in archaeology and digital humanities. This section identifies the
principal risks and suggests practical responses.

\subsection{Coordinate Choices Construct Alignment Signals}

Any spatial scoring method that measures distances or alignments in geographic
coordinates implicitly assumes that the coordinate system is meaningful for
the cultural process under study. In most cases, this assumption is
not examined. The harmonic-deviation framework makes the dependence visible
because it selects a frame by optimization --- but the same representational
dependence is present in, for example, nearest-site distances, kernel density
estimates in longitude-latitude space, or quadrant-count statistics defined
relative to an arbitrary grid.

When a corpus is geographically clustered, coordinate-aligned statistics
find alignment signals at the scale of the clusters, because lattice nodes or
kernel centers coincide with cluster locations for some parameterizations, and
the optimizing procedure selects the most favorable one. This is not a subtle
effect: it produces multiple diagnostics all pointing in the same direction ---
as our three primary tests demonstrate --- while the underlying alignment is
entirely framework-induced.

\subsection{Risks in Spatial Analysis of Heritage Corpora}

Cultural heritage datasets are almost always spatially clustered, for reasons
that have nothing to do with the cultural processes under investigation:
inscription history, institutional capacity, geographic coverage of field
survey, preservation conditions, and political factors all shape which sites
are included. These corpus-construction effects create spatial structure that
a coordinate-aligned scoring method detects as a meaningful alignment signal.

Specific risks include:

\begin{itemize}

\item \textbf{Phase-selection bias.} When a scoring framework optimizes over
reference phases or anchor points, the optimum will reflect the corpus
structure rather than any physical signal. The anchor-sweep correction
in this paper (marginal $p = \anchorMaxPermP$ after full search)
illustrates how quickly the correction exhausts the signal.

\item \textbf{Spacing selection.} The optimal spacing recovered by an
unconstrained sweep will tend toward large values (because large spacings
have artificially low null rates under fixed-threshold statistics) or toward
values that match the inter-cluster spacing. Neither is likely to reflect
a culturally meaningful period.

\item \textbf{Cross-corpus consistency without independence.} When multiple
corpora share geographic and cultural structure --- as UNESCO, Wikidata
heritage inventories, and trade route databases do for the Eurasian corridor
--- showing that the same alignment signal appears in all of them under the
same representation is not independent replication. It is
shared-representation consistency. The distinction is consequential for how
results are presented and interpreted.

\item \textbf{Resampling as confirmation.} Permutation and bootstrap tests
confirm that an alignment signal is robust to resampling from the observed
corpus. They do not confirm that the signal would be observed in a differently
structured corpus, under a different scoring method, or under invariant
spectral analysis. Positive resampling results within a representation-dependent
framework are necessary but not sufficient to conclude that intrinsic
structure exists.

\end{itemize}

\subsection{Methodological Recommendations}

Three practical responses follow from the present case study.

\textbf{Include invariant comparison tests.} Any spatial analysis using
coordinate-aligned scoring should be accompanied by at least one invariant
test --- a spectral method, a Ripley $K$-function analysis, or a
rotation-invariant distance statistic --- that does not assume a reference
frame. Agreement between the two classes of test provides stronger evidence
than either alone; disagreement (as in this paper) indicates that the observed
signal is representation-dependent.

\textbf{Report the clustering structure of the corpus.} The geographic
distribution of the corpus is part of the analysis context, not just
background. When a corpus is strongly clustered, the expected behavior of
coordinate-aligned statistics under the null should be assessed using a
corpus-realistic null (such as our Null~C) rather than a uniform or
longitude-permutation null.

\textbf{Distinguish optimization from discovery.} When a scoring framework
selects parameters by optimizing over the data, the selected frame is a
property of the optimization, not evidence of a pre-existing structure.
Post-selection inference requires explicit correction; targeted tests at the
selected frame provide within-frame calibration of the alignment signal,
not confirmatory discovery of intrinsic periodicity.

\subsection{Broader Relevance to Digital Humanities}

The mechanisms identified here are not specific to harmonic-alignment scoring
or to monument distributions. Any computational method that maps a cultural
corpus onto a coordinate space and measures deviations from a reference
pattern is subject to the same representation-dependence risks. This includes
cultural geography analyses of textual production, temporal clustering in
publication or inscription records, and network analyses whose spatial
embedding reflects geographic coordinates. The UNESCO case study provides
a documented, reproducible example of a representation-specific alignment
signal in a high-profile public dataset, which can serve as a reference case
for methodological training and preregistration design in the digital
humanities.

The broader implication for the cultural analytics literature is direct.
Published spatial analyses of cultural and archaeological data regularly
report alignment patterns, clustering signals, and periodicity results based
on coordinate-aligned scoring methods. Some reported alignment signals
reflect intrinsic spatial structure; others arise from the interaction of
corpus clustering, coordinate framing, and scoring function demonstrated here.
The two cases are difficult to distinguish without an invariant comparison.
The present study makes the distinction formally, by showing that the signal
passes multiple resampling tests within the representation and fails a single
invariant test outside it. Researchers evaluating spatial alignment claims in
the literature should ask: was an invariant comparison performed, and does
the corpus structure --- particularly its geographic clustering --- predict
the reported signal under the scoring framework's null?

% ==============================================================================
\section{Conclusion}
\label{sec:conclusion}
% ==============================================================================

We have demonstrated that a harmonic-deviation scoring framework applied to
the UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World Heritage corpus produces a reproducible,
phase-localized alignment signal within the selected harmonic representation.
The signal is:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Empirically reproducible within the representation}: three
  threshold-free diagnostics converge (Holm-adjusted $p_{\mathrm{adj}} =
  \pHolmTestOne$, $\pHolmTestTwo$, $\pHolmTestThree$); leave-$n$-out checks
  confirm stability; multi-scale enrichment is consistent across proximity
  windows.

\item \textbf{Cross-dataset consistent}: the alignment signal appears under
  the same harmonic-deviation representation in the Wikidata Q180987 stupa
  corpus and OWTRAD route network, reflecting the shared Eurasian geographic
  structure of all three datasets.

\item \textbf{Representation-induced}: the signal does not appear under
  invariant spectral analysis ($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$,
  \fullRayleighPermPSig{} for the full-corpus Rayleigh test); it is sensitive
  to phase and spacing perturbations; and it is absent in the Americas
  negative control, which lacks the Eurasian clustering that drives the
  effect within the scoring framework.
\end{itemize}

The contribution of this study is methodological. It demonstrates, with a
fully reproducible pipeline on a major public corpus, that coordinate-dependent
spatial scoring constructs stable, cross-dataset consistent alignment signals
in geographically clustered cultural heritage data. The construction mechanism
--- the interaction of corpus clustering with coordinate framing and scoring
function choice --- is transparent and generalizable. It applies to any
spatial analysis in which the corpus has non-uniform geographic coverage, the
scoring method is anchored to a coordinate system, and the frame is selected
rather than pre-specified.

The mechanism is transparent and generalizable: geographic clustering, a
coordinate-anchored scoring function, and frame optimization jointly construct
a stable alignment signal wherever these three ingredients co-occur.
Identifying the mechanism, demonstrating it on a major public corpus, and
formally separating it from intrinsic periodicity via invariant comparison
--- this constitutes the contribution of the study. The appropriate next step
is to apply invariant methods and corpus-realistic nulls as standard components
of spatial analysis workflows in cultural analytics, so that framework-induced
alignment signals are detected before, not after, interpretive conclusions are
drawn.

% ==============================================================================
%  SUPPLEMENTARY NOTE
% ==============================================================================

\paragraph{Data and code availability.}
\label{sec:data_code}
All analysis scripts, data files, and reproduction instructions are archived
at Zenodo (DOI: \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19938283}, v1.6.0).
The pipeline regenerates all statistics and LaTeX macros from raw data
without manual editing (\texttt{bash manuscript/reproduce\_all\_macros.sh}).
Full audit files, keyword lists, and supplementary figures are provided in
Supplementary~S1.

% ==============================================================================
%  APPENDIX
% ==============================================================================

\appendix

\section{Tier-Threshold Sensitivity}
\label{app:tier_sensitivity}

A log-scale null-rate sweep across \logSweepNtaus{} thresholds shows that
the alignment signal is not an artifact of the specific tier width:
\logSweepNsig{} of \logSweepNtaus{} thresholds reach $p < 0.05$ for the
dome subset within the harmonic representation; dome OR ranges
\logSweepMinEnrichCanon{}$\times$--\logSweepMaxEnrichCanon{}$\times$ across
the three canonical tiers. Within $\pm$1 log-decade of the A+ threshold,
\logSweepAregionNsig{} of \logSweepAregionNtaus{} thresholds are significant.

\section{Von~Mises Mixture Characterization of External Corpora}
\label{app:vonmises}

A von~Mises + uniform mixture fitted to the harmonic phase deviations of
external corpora under the selected representation:
OWTRAD route nodes ($N = \vmNOwtrad{}$):
$\hat{\kappa} = \vmKappaOwtrad{}$, $\hat{w} = \vmMixtureWeightOwtrad{}$,
$\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegOwtrad{}^\circ$.
Wikidata Q180987 stupas ($N = \vmNStupa{}$):
$\hat{\kappa} = \vmKappaStupa{}$, $\hat{w} = \vmMixtureWeightStupa{}$,
$\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegStupa{}^\circ$.
$N$-weighted pooled $\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegPooled{}^\circ$;
$\kappa$ ratio $= \vmKappaRatio{}$ across corpora.
These values characterize external-corpus clustering within the harmonic
representation and are not used to derive tier boundaries.

\section{Keyword Audit}
\label{app:keywords}

\NcircKeywords{} morphological keywords from four roots (\textit{stupa/stupas},
\textit{tholos}, \textit{dome/domed/domes}, \textit{spherical}) matched as
whole words against site names, XML descriptions, and extended OUV text.
Context-validation for ambiguous terms: Gate~1 (\NdomeGateNeg{} negative
patterns) rejects non-architectural uses; Gate~2 (\NdomeGatePos{} positive
patterns) requires architectural confirmation.
Seven sites rejected in the context-validated subset:
Hiroshima Peace Memorial, Richtersveld, Gyeongju,
Vi\~{n}ales Valley, Rock Islands Southern Lagoon, Uluru--Kata Tju\d{t}a,
and Diqu\'{i}s Stone Spheres.
Full site-by-site audit in the Zenodo archive \citep{tenenbaum2026audit}.
